$schema_note: Sprint brief for the Sanders Intelligence app. One file per micro-sprint (1 day cadence). Each task is a self-contained implementation prompt: outline, technical requirements, expected results, test coverage. Generated by the sprint-planner skill.
theme: Close out task-workflow gaps (auto-assign, table view, no-impact tracking) + clear the overdue F5 freeze bug + UX cleanup for auto-task descriptions

project.name: Sanders intelligence app
project.stack: React 18 + TypeScript + Vite, Tailwind, React Router v6, TanStack Query v5, Supabase, Recharts

selection_rationale: Yesterday's sprint shipped 5 of 6 (Daily View, group-by-Assignee, comments, postpone, Excess Value bug fix). Auto-assign (1215179791172802) is the one carry-over and the prerequisite for the Assignee view to deliver value. The newest backlog item (1215237856902619, table view with adjustable columns, added today) gets the recency boost and pairs naturally with the task-workflow theme. The F5 freeze bug (1215143557131647) is OVERDUE and has been open 3 days - pulling it in to keep the bug list short. Wall-of-text descriptions (1215225992585074) is the UX polish that complements the table view. No-impact-completion tracking (1215103753564376) is the natural next slice on the just-shipped Daily View.
instructions_for_implementer: Work tasks in the 'order' field. For EACH task: read 'clarifying_questions' first and ask the user before writing code. Treat 'likely_files' as starting points for investigation, not the full surface. Implement against 'requirements', validate against 'completion_criteria', and write/extend tests per 'test_coverage'. When done, update the matching Asana task's completion status and leave a comment citing the file/line evidence (mirror the convention used when closing 1215143640254108).

carried_over: 1215179791172802 - Auto-assign system tasks (only sprint-2026-05-28 pick that did not ship; SQL line 411 in migration 006 still hardcodes assigned_to = null)
tasks_closed_since_prior_sprint: 1215143640254108 - BUG: Excess Value mismatch (closed 2026-05-29; sumExcessValue helper landed)
1215143337734271 - financialMetrics tests (closed 2026-05-29; financialMetrics.test.ts created alongside the bug fix)
newly_added_since_prior_sprint: 1215237856902619 - FEATURE: Tasks table view with adjustable columns (added 2026-05-29)
deferred_to_next_sprint: 1215210678717164 - Daily View claim flow for unassigned tasks (pushed to 2026-06-02; becomes largely redundant once 1215179791172802 ships, will reassess after)
1215223368364542 - Apostrophe in Daily View subtitle (10-minute fix; tag onto any TasksPage / DailyView touch this sprint instead of carving out a slot)

tasks.title: BUG: Stop Action Center renderer from freezing on F5 reload
tasks.outline: Pressing F5 on /purchasing/action-center wedges the renderer for >20 seconds (Chrome DevTools Page.captureScreenshot times out at 30s). Recoverable by clicking another nav link, but unprofessional and worse on slower machines. Filed 2026-05-26, reproduced 2026-05-28 and 2026-05-29. The bug has been open the longest; clear it.
tasks.technical_description:
useInventory() re-fetches all 12,805 records on a hard reload, then a downstream component re-renders synchronously and starves the main thread. Most likely culprit: ActionCenter's grouped table builds a derived structure (vendor -> category -> SKUs) that allocates a large object graph on every render, plus renders ~600 visible rows without virtualization. The freeze is during the FIRST render after data arrival, not during the fetch itself.

Two fix paths:
(a) Virtualize the long table with react-window or @tanstack/react-virtual. Best for the long term; biggest perf gain.
(b) Chunk the row enrichment with useDeferredValue or startTransition so the heavy work doesn't block paint. Smaller change, easier rollback.

Recommend (b) for this sprint - the freeze appears to be the synchronous derivation in ActionCenter.helpers.ts rather than the row count itself. Confirm with a React DevTools Profiler run before choosing.
likely_files: app/src/pages/purchasing/ActionCenter.tsx - main render + useMemo chains that build the grouped structure
app/src/pages/purchasing/ActionCenter.helpers.ts - the grouping + dedupe pipeline (probably the hot spot)
app/src/hooks/useInventory.ts - confirm refetch behavior on hard reload; possibly add staleTime / placeholderData
app/src/__tests__/ActionCenter.helpers.test.ts - extend with a perf-style assertion (input row count -> output ready within N ms on the test bed, loose threshold)
requirements: Profile first: open the React DevTools Profiler, hard-reload /purchasing/action-center, capture the flame chart, identify the component(s) responsible for >50% of the post-fetch render time.

Apply the lightest fix that eliminates the >20s freeze. Acceptable targets: (a) wrap heavy useMemo / derivation in useDeferredValue or React.startTransition, (b) split the render into chunks via virtualization, (c) hoist precomputation into a Web Worker - last resort.
Add a useInventory staleTime of at least 30s so a hard reload while data is fresh in cache doesn't trigger a full refetch.
Confirm the fix on Chrome AND verify there's no regression on Firefox / Safari render paths.
completion_criteria: F5 on /purchasing/action-center renders the page (KPI cards + first row of the Attention Required table) within 5 seconds, measured cold (no warm cache).
Chrome DevTools 'Page.captureScreenshot' completes within 2 seconds during the post-reload window.
No new console errors, no TypeScript errors, all existing ActionCenter.helpers tests still pass.
Inventory Browser F5 (also heavy) also benefits or at least does not regress - confirm by manual reload.
expected_outcomes: User who hits F5 (the most common page-refresh action) no longer thinks the app crashed. Lowers support friction and a frustration point that has been reported 3 separate QA runs.

unit: If ActionCenter.helpers exposes the heavy pipeline as a pure function, add a benchmark-flavored test: given a fixture of 12,000 rows, the pipeline completes in < 250ms on the CI test bed (Vitest's bench API or a simple performance.now() assertion).
Test that the deferred path renders an interim 'computing...' state when the heavy derivation is in flight (if that's the chosen fix).
integration: RTL render ActionCenter with a 12k-row fixture; assert the first KPI card appears in the DOM within 3 seconds (jsdom is fast; this is a coarse smoke check, not a real perf test).
manual_qa: F5 on Action Center with the full production dataset (12,805 rows). Confirm interactive (sidebar clickable, scroll responsive) within 5 seconds.
Same test on Inventory Browser (also 12,805 rows) - no regression.

clarifying_questions: Acceptable to add a 'Loading inventory data...' placeholder during the deferred render, or must the page transition cleanly without an interstitial?
Virtualization library preference (react-window vs @tanstack/react-virtual) if path (a) wins after profiling? Recommendation: @tanstack/react-virtual - same ecosystem as TanStack Query.
Is there a fixed perf budget? Recommendation: 'interactive in under 5s on the standard dev machine' as the soft target.
tasks.pre_implementation_instruction: Profile first. Do not start optimizing without the flame chart - the hot spot is not necessarily what we'd guess.

tasks.title: Auto-assign system-created tasks to the purchasing head (carry-over)
tasks.outline: Carry-over from sprint 2026-05-28. Auto-task creation went live but tasks land Unassigned (verified: migration 006 line 411 hardcodes assigned_to = null). Until this ships, the Assignee group view that we DID ship shows one giant 'Unassigned' bucket with 27 tasks. Tiny SQL change unlocks the value of three other features that have already shipped.
tasks.technical_description:
Two pieces:
(1) automation_config table (already exists, migration 006) needs a default_auto_assignee_id column referencing public.users(id). Add a new migration 009_auto_task_default_assignee.sql for the schema change + a backfill statement setting it to Mordechai Rub's user id (or NULL if not configured, in which case we fall back).
(2) The generate_auto_tasks_for_upload() function in migration 006 needs to read default_auto_assignee_id from automation_config and pass it into the tasks INSERT at line 411. Add a fallback: if the configured user is missing or inactive, leave assigned_to NULL (current behavior) so we never crash the upload pipeline.
likely_files: supabase/migrations/009_auto_task_default_assignee.sql - NEW: ALTER automation_config ADD COLUMN default_auto_assignee_id uuid references public.users(id); seed the column with the configured user.

supabase/migrations/006_auto_task_gating.sql - DO NOT EDIT (migrations are immutable). The new logic lives in migration 009: it can DROP and CREATE OR REPLACE the generate_auto_tasks_for_upload function with the assigned_to-aware version.

app/src/pages/admin/UsersPage.tsx - small UI addition: a 'Default auto-assignee' badge or radio per user row, calling a useSetDefaultAutoAssignee mutation. Optional this sprint; can defer if scope tight.

app/src/__tests__/autoTaskAssignment.test.ts - NEW integration test (or extension of an existing one) that simulates the generate_auto_tasks_for_upload path and asserts assigned_to is set when config is populated, NULL when not.
requirements: Add default_auto_assignee_id uuid column to automation_config (referencing public.users), nullable.
Migration must seed the value for the 'auto_tasks' config row with the purchasing-head user id - take this id from the user as a clarifying-question answer (NOT hardcode it).

generate_auto_tasks_for_upload() reads the default_auto_assignee_id and passes it to the tasks INSERT. If the value is NULL or the referenced user is no longer active (is_active = false), pass NULL (preserve current behavior).
No regression to manually-created tasks (TaskModal still allows assignee selection unchanged).
Existing 27 unassigned auto-tasks: leave them alone OR run a one-time backfill statement assigning them to the configured user - confirm with user.
Re-running the upload pipeline after this migration creates tasks WITH assigned_to populated.
completion_criteria: Trigger a fresh CSV upload that satisfies an auto-task rule. The resulting task on /tasks shows the configured user in the Assignee column.
On /tasks, switching to Assignee group mode shows two buckets (the configured user + Unassigned for any tasks predating the migration, or just one bucket if backfill ran).
Sign in as the configured user, navigate to /daily - the auto-tasks appear under 'Today' and the 'Due today' counter is non-zero.
If the user is deactivated via UsersPage and a new upload runs, tasks fall back to Unassigned (no crash).
expected_outcomes: Three already-shipped features start delivering full value: Assignee group view becomes useful, /daily stops showing 0/0/0 for the purchasing head, and the 'who owns this' question has an answer the moment a task is created.

unit: If UsersPage gains the toggle: app/src/__tests__/UsersPage.helpers.test.ts asserts the mutation payload shape (config key, user_id).
integration: Add a test (Vitest + supabase mock or msw) that calls the generate_auto_tasks_for_upload-equivalent path and asserts the INSERT receives assigned_to = configured user id when populated, NULL when not.
manual_qa: Set the default assignee to a user, run an upload, confirm tasks land with that user.
Deactivate the user, run another upload, confirm tasks land Unassigned (no error).
Reactivate, run another upload, confirm tasks land back with that user.

clarifying_questions: Confirm the default assignee: Mordechai Rub (gid visible on /admin/users)? Or someone else?
Should we backfill the existing 27 unassigned auto-tasks to the configured user, or leave them alone (only new uploads get the new behavior)? Recommendation: backfill; cleaner state.

Add the 'Default auto-assignee' UI on UsersPage this sprint, or do it as a DB-only change for now and ship the toggle later? Recommendation: DB-only this sprint, UI in a follow-up - keeps scope tight.
tasks.pre_implementation_instruction: Ask the clarifying questions and wait for explicit direction before editing files. The default assignee identity blocks the migration.

tasks.title: Tasks page - table view with adjustable columns + add-column picker
tasks.outline: New backlog ask added 2026-05-29: in addition to the existing Board and List views on /tasks, add a Table view with sortable columns and a 'Choose columns' button so users can show/hide fields (priority, due date, assignee, SKU, vendor, comment count, etc). With 27 auto-tasks now live and growing, the wall-of-text card layout is no longer the right shape; a dense table is.
tasks.technical_description:
TasksPage.tsx already has a view-mode state (currently 'board' | 'list'). Extend it to 'board' | 'list' | 'table'. Add a Table view component that renders one row per task with the configurable columns. Persist column visibility + order in localStorage so it survives page reloads. Use a small column-config map keyed by column id, similar to how the existing SortableTh / sort state works on /purchasing/inventory.

Use the existing visual language: tabular-nums, badge variants for status/priority, tight row height (no card padding), striped or hover-highlight rows. Reuse the existing useTasks + useTaskCommentCounts hooks - no new data layer needed.
likely_files: app/src/pages/tasks/TasksPage.tsx - extend view-mode state + toolbar toggle
app/src/pages/tasks/TasksTable.tsx - NEW component, the table renderer (separate file keeps TasksPage manageable)
app/src/pages/tasks/TasksTable.helpers.ts - NEW: COLUMN_DEFS (id, label, accessor, sortable, defaultVisible, width), getVisibleColumns(prefs), sortRows(rows, sortState)
app/src/components/tasks/ColumnPicker.tsx - NEW: dropdown / popover with one toggle per column + drag-handles for reorder
app/src/__tests__/TasksTable.helpers.test.ts - NEW tests for column visibility persistence + sort
requirements: Add a 'Table' button next to Board / List in the TasksPage toolbar. Selected state styled like the existing toggles.

Table columns (default visible): Title, Status, Priority, Due Date, Assignee, Vendor, Comments (count). Available-but-hidden: SKU, Department, Created at, Created by, Source (manual / auto), Postponed until.
Click any column header to sort ascending; click again to sort descending; click a third time to clear sort. SortableTh pattern from /purchasing/vendors.

'Columns' button opens a popover with one checkbox per column. Saving persists the visible set to localStorage under key 'tasks.table.columns.v1'. On load, hydrate state from localStorage with the default set as a fallback.
Department-filter and Assignee-group-mode controls remain in the toolbar but ONLY apply to List and Board views (Table is its own dimension; grouping doesn't make sense for a table).
Row click opens the existing TaskModal (no fork).
Empty state ('No tasks') matches the List view's empty state styling.
completion_criteria: On /tasks, clicking 'Table' shows a table with one row per task using the default columns.
Hiding a column via Columns picker, reloading the page, and confirming the hidden state persists.
Sorting by Due Date ascending puts overdue + nearest-due at top; clicking again puts furthest-out at top.
Clicking a row opens TaskModal pre-populated with that task's data.
Switching to Board or List and back preserves the user's column choices.
No console errors, no TypeScript errors, all existing TasksPage tests still pass.
expected_outcomes: Power users (purchasing leads scanning 27+ auto-tasks) get a dense, sortable, filterable view that respects their column preferences. Reduces the visual noise of the card layout while keeping it available for users who prefer it.

unit: - getVisibleColumns returns the default set when localStorage is empty
- sortRows handles asc / desc / cleared for each column type (string, number, date, nullable)
- Nullable columns sort with nulls last in both directions (matches the codebase convention from VendorView)
integration: RTL render TasksTable with a 5-row fixture, click a column header, assert the order changes.
Open ColumnPicker, uncheck Vendor, close popover, assert Vendor column disappears from the DOM and the preference persists across a re-render.
manual_qa: Click through all three view modes (Board / List / Table) - confirm no console errors.
Hide all columns - the table should show only Title (or an empty state, designer's call).
Hide Comments column, save, reload page (F5), confirm the choice persists.

clarifying_questions: Default visible columns - confirm the recommended set (Title, Status, Priority, Due Date, Assignee, Vendor, Comments) or swap?
Should the table view be the default when there are >N tasks (e.g., auto-promote to table at 15+), or always opt-in?
Column reordering via drag-handles in v1, or text-list-with-arrows? Recommendation: skip drag in v1, just toggle visibility, ship faster.
Bulk actions (multi-select rows + bulk postpone / assign) - in scope this sprint or follow-up?
tasks.pre_implementation_instruction: Ask the clarifying questions and wait for explicit direction before editing files. The default-column-set and bulk-actions decisions materially affect scope.

tasks.title: UX: Auto-task descriptions - move metadata out of description into structured fields
tasks.outline: Auto-task descriptions are walls of text like 'Vendor: X Rule: Y Upload: <uuid> (timestamp) - SKU-1: change - SKU-2: change - ...'. Hard to scan, eats vertical space on TaskCard, and the upload UUID is meaningless to humans. The new Table view (task #3) is the natural moment to split metadata into typed columns and let the description go back to being a clean prose summary.
tasks.technical_description:
Today's auto-task generator (DB function in migration 006) emits all metadata as a string concatenated into the description field. Migration 010 (NEW) adds typed columns to public.tasks: rule_id (text), vendor_supplier_code (text), affected_skus (text[]), upload_id (uuid). Update the DB function to populate these columns. Update the description to a short human summary: '<N> SKUs <newly at risk | newly excess | with COGS rising> for <vendor>'.

Client-side: types/index.ts gets the new fields; TaskCard / Table view / TaskModal can read them directly instead of regex-parsing the description.

Backfill: a one-time SQL statement that parses the existing 27 auto-task descriptions and populates the new columns. Acceptable to skip if user prefers to discard old auto-tasks.
likely_files: supabase/migrations/010_auto_task_structured_metadata.sql - NEW: ALTER tasks ADD COLUMN rule_id text, vendor_supplier_code text, affected_skus text[], upload_id uuid; backfill from existing descriptions; CREATE OR REPLACE generate_auto_tasks_for_upload with the new writer.
app/src/types/index.ts - extend Task interface
app/src/components/tasks/TaskCard.tsx - render rule + vendor chips instead of description preview; collapse SKU list to '<N> SKUs' with click-to-expand
app/src/pages/tasks/TasksTable.helpers.ts - add Rule and Vendor as table columns (from task #3)
app/src/components/tasks/TaskModal.tsx - render the structured metadata in a tidy block separate from the prose description
app/src/__tests__/TaskCard.static.test.ts - extend with structured-metadata fixtures
requirements: Migration adds the 4 new columns. All nullable (manually-created tasks have NULL).
DB function backfills 27 existing auto-tasks by parsing the description. Acceptable to leave un-parseable ones alone.
DB function for new auto-tasks writes to the structured columns AND a short prose summary in description ('5 SKUs at risk for Linen Choice-FMT').
Client-side renderers prefer structured fields when present; gracefully degrade to the description if the fields are null (covers manual tasks).
TaskCard for auto-tasks: vendor chip + rule chip + '<N> SKUs - click to expand' (expansion opens TaskModal).
No regression to manual tasks - their description is unchanged.
completion_criteria: 27 existing auto-tasks render with the new tight TaskCard layout after the backfill runs.
A new CSV upload generates an auto-task with both the structured columns and the prose summary populated.
Manually-created tasks (TaskModal) render unchanged.
Table view (task #3) can render Rule and Vendor columns sourced from the structured fields.
No console errors, no TypeScript errors, all existing tests still pass.
expected_outcomes: Task list reading speed jumps. The page no longer feels like a log file. Pairs with the table view to give purchasing a scannable, prioritizable queue.

unit: TaskCard.static.test.ts: with a fixture having structured columns, renders the chips + truncated SKU summary; with a fixture having only description (manual task), renders the description preview.
If a parser helper is extracted: test the parse-from-description path with a real fixture from the 27 existing auto-tasks.
integration: DB-level test of the backfill: insert a fixture description, run the backfill statement, assert the columns are populated correctly. (Pair with the auto-task DB function test from 1215225992307667.)
manual_qa: Visit /tasks, scan the 27 auto-tasks - confirm the new compact layout reads cleanly.
Click an auto-task card - TaskModal opens with the full SKU list visible.
Trigger a fresh CSV upload, confirm the resulting task has both the structured fields and the prose summary.

clarifying_questions: Backfill existing 27 auto-tasks or leave them as-is and let them age out? Recommendation: backfill - cleaner state, low risk.
Prose summary template: '5 SKUs at risk for Vendor X' vs 'Inventory review: 5 SKUs at Vendor X newly at risk' - which reads better?
Keep the upload_id and timestamp on the card or hide them behind 'View metadata' in TaskModal? Recommendation: hide; they're audit trail, not decision-making info.
tasks.pre_implementation_instruction: Ask the clarifying questions and wait for explicit direction before editing files. Coordinate with task #3 - the table view's Rule/Vendor columns depend on these structured fields landing.

tasks.title: Daily View - 'Reopened' section for tasks completed without data impact
tasks.outline: When a user marks an auto-task done but the underlying inventory condition didn't actually change in the next upload, the system re-creates the same task (or it should). Surface those re-emissions on /daily so users see 'you closed this yesterday but it came back today - your action didn't stick' and have a clear next step.
tasks.technical_description:
The auto-task creation function in migration 006 already detects when a previously-created auto-task is still applicable and decides whether to suppress (line 386-388, status='todo' or 'in_progress' wins) vs create a fresh one. The signal we need: 'this task was previously closed (done OR cancelled) but the condition re-triggered'. Migration 011 (NEW) adds a `reopened_from_task_id uuid references public.tasks(id)` column on tasks. The DB function sets it when the auto-task generator re-creates a previously-closed task for the same event_key.

Client-side: DailyView gets a new 'Came back' section between Today and Completed Yesterday: tasks where reopened_from_task_id is not null AND created_at >= today. Shows the original closure date + comment (from task_comments where kind in 'completion' or 'postpone').
likely_files: supabase/migrations/011_task_reopened_tracking.sql - NEW: ALTER tasks ADD COLUMN reopened_from_task_id uuid references public.tasks(id); update generate_auto_tasks_for_upload() to set it when the same event_key signature was closed previously
app/src/types/index.ts - Task interface gets reopened_from_task_id
app/src/pages/work/DailyView.helpers.ts - new partition function isReopenedToday(task, today)
app/src/pages/work/DailyView.tsx - new section between Today and Completed yesterday
app/src/__tests__/DailyView.helpers.test.ts - extend with reopened-tasks fixtures
requirements: Migration adds reopened_from_task_id column. Nullable; only set for auto-tasks where the previous task with the same event_key was closed (done OR cancelled, not postponed).
DB function: when about to create an auto-task, check for a previous closed task with the same event_key in the last 30 days. If found, set reopened_from_task_id to that task's id.

DailyView renders a new section 'Came back' (or 'Reopened today' - confirm copy) ordered between Today and Completed Yesterday. Section shows: original closure date, original closure comment (if any), priority, vendor.
Section hides when empty (no noise on quiet days).
Clicking a reopened task opens the TaskModal with the original task's Timeline events visible (the comment thread already shows the closure note from when it was marked done).
Counter card: optionally a 4th counter 'Came back' on the KPI row - clarify before adding.
completion_criteria: Close an auto-task (mark done). Force a fresh upload that still satisfies the rule. Confirm: a new task is created, the new task has reopened_from_task_id set to the closed one, and the new task appears in the 'Came back' section on /daily.
Same scenario but the rule is no longer satisfied - no new task created, no reopened entry.
Postponed tasks: closing-and-coming-back doesn't trigger the reopened flag (postpone has its own suppression).
DailyView empty state for the section reads cleanly when no reopened tasks exist.
No console errors, no TypeScript errors, all existing DailyView tests still pass.
expected_outcomes: Users catch 'I checked the box but didn't actually fix it' patterns quickly. Tightens the auto-task feedback loop and stops the silent re-emergence of unaddressed work.

unit: DailyView.helpers.test.ts: partitionDailyTasks correctly bins reopened tasks into the new bucket; ignores them in the standard 'Today' bucket to avoid double-counting (or counts them in both - clarify).
DB-level: assert generate_auto_tasks_for_upload sets reopened_from_task_id when a prior task with the same event_key exists in status done.
integration: RTL render DailyView with a fixture including a reopened task; assert the 'Came back' section renders and the original closure note appears.
manual_qa: End-to-end: mark an auto-task done, re-upload the same CSV, refresh /daily, confirm the task appears in the reopened section with the original closure timestamp.

clarifying_questions: Section title: 'Came back', 'Reopened today', 'Recurring issues'? Recommendation: 'Came back' - human and short.
Count toward 'Created today' counter? Recommendation: NO - these are recurring tasks, not new work; double-counting would mislead.
Should we extend this to cancelled tasks too, or only completed ones? Cancelled = 'I'm choosing not to do this'; if it comes back, that's a re-prompt. Recommendation: yes, include cancelled.
30-day lookback window for matching event_key - too short? too long? Recommendation: 30 days matches the existing financialMetrics 30d window, easy to reason about.
tasks.pre_implementation_instruction: Ask the clarifying questions and wait for explicit direction before editing files. Section title and counter-double-count decisions affect copy and downstream metrics.

sprint_close_checklist: All 5 sprint tasks marked completed in Asana with a comment citing file paths and line numbers (mirror the close convention used 2026-05-29).
All new tests pass: cd app && npm test.

Run cd app && npm run dev and smoke-test: F5 on Action Center (no freeze), /tasks Table view + column picker + persistence, auto-tasks now show assignee, auto-task descriptions render compact, /daily 'Came back' section renders end-to-end.
Update CLAUDE.md: new auto_task structured columns, new tasks.reopened_from_task_id column, new tasks Table view under Page Map.
Update audit_map.md in the sprint-planner skill: add rows for TasksTable.helpers.ts, ColumnPicker.tsx, and the structured-metadata fields on tasks.
Update memory project_sanders_intelligence.md if the auto-task data model changed materially (new columns, new lookback windows).
